title: Reading and Use of English – Part 2
description: For questions 9–16, read the text below and think of the word which best fits each gap. Use only ONE word in each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).

content.text: Time is a concept that we all understand intuitively, (0) yet defining it scientifically remains one of the greatest challenges in physics. (9) Although we perceive time as a constant flow from the past to the future, Einstein’s theory of relativity suggests otherwise. (10) Such is the influence of gravity and velocity that time can actually slow down or speed up depending on one's position. (11) Provided that an observer travels at speeds approaching that of light, they would experience time much more slowly than someone on Earth. (12) Not only does this phenomenon challenge our daily experience, but it also forces us to rethink the very structure of the universe. (13) Regardless of how solid our perception of 'now' feels, many physicists argue that the past, present, and future all exist simultaneously. (14) Not until the development of atomic clocks were scientists able to measure these infinitesimal differences in time with absolute precision. (15) Despite these discoveries, the fundamental reason why time appears to move in only one direction remains a mystery. (16) Whether or not we will ever truly master the secrets of time is a question that continues to inspire both science and philosophy.

example.text: Time is a concept that we all understand intuitively, (0) yet defining it scientifically remains one of the greatest challenges.
example.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrasting fact.

questions.explanation: Conjunction used to introduce a contrast or concession.
Determiner used for emphasis at the start of the clause.
Part of the 'Not only... but also' correlative conjunction.
Part of the fixed phrase 'Regardless of'.
Part of the 'Not until' structure requiring inversion ('were scientists able').
Preposition used to introduce a contrast.
Conjunction introducing two possibilities or a doubt.
